context: The entire piece is built on D Dorian for the A section and Eb Dorian for the B section. The iconic 'So What' chord is a drop 2 voicing of a minor 7th. This is the definitive example of modal jazz — harmony that doesn't move by function but by color.

artist: Standard (Miles Davis / Bill Evans versions recommended)
context: A textbook example of ii-V-I chains moving through related keys. The first half cycles through G major, the second through G minor. Perfect for hearing ii-V-I in both major and minor contexts back to back.

The most harmonically complex standard in the jazz repertoire. The changes cycle through three key centers (B, G, Eb) a major third apart — dividing the octave into three equal parts. Every chord is approached by a ii-V from a tritone away. Terrifying to improvise over, beautiful to analyze.

The bridge contains a beautiful tritone substitution where a Db7 replaces the expected G7, creating a chromatic bass line descending into the tonic. A gentle entry point for hearing tritone subs in a familiar context.

artist: Standard (Charlie Parker, Bill Evans versions recommended)
context: Moves through six key centers in 36 bars. Bebop musicians used it as a vehicle for tritone substitutions and chromatic lines. Learning to navigate 'All The Things' is a rite of passage in jazz. The opening four chords (Fm7-Bb7-Ebmaj7-Abmaj7) are a ii-V-I extended by a IV chord.

One of the most beautiful and accessible jazz standards for hearing ii-V-I in a relaxed context. The opening bars spell out Ebmaj7 then move through a series of ii-V-Is. The melody sits on major 7th intervals — that dreamy, suspended quality is the major 7th chord tone.

artist: Standard (Bill Evans, Miles Davis versions recommended)
context: Begins ambiguously — the first chord (Em7b5 to A7) implies C minor before resolving to Bb major. The changes constantly shift between major and minor key centers. Bill Evans' harmonizations are masterclasses in chord voicing and reharmonization.

The verse sits on a pedal note while the harmony shifts above it — a tritone away from the eventual tonic. Porter's harmonic language was ahead of its time. The resolution from the verse's pedal to the chorus tonic is deeply satisfying and theoretically elegant.

The bridge modulates to a key a half step up using enharmonic equivalence — a move that influenced bebop harmony enormously. Coleman Hawkins' 1939 recording is widely considered the first bebop performance.

Essentially 'So What' reharmonized. D Dorian for the A section, Eb Dorian for the bridge. Coltrane's solo is a clinic in developing a short motif across a modal landscape — he'll take a 3-note idea and transform it for minutes at a time.

A two-chord vamp: Am7 to D7. That's the Dorian mode signature — a minor chord with a major IV chord. The D7 (not Dm7) is what gives Dorian its brightness compared to natural minor. Santana's guitar tone on this track is pure Dorian pentatonic.

A clear example of Dorian mode in a folk context. The raised 6th degree is audible throughout the melody — it's what gives the song its slightly ancient, ambiguous quality. Compare it to a natural minor melody and the difference in color is immediately obvious.

Pure Aeolian (natural minor). The string arrangement oscillates between Em and C, never resolving to a major chord. The absence of bass guitar and drums creates harmonic starkness. A beautiful example of how staying in a mode rather than using functional harmony creates a specific emotional color.

The verse sits in E Mixolydian — the flat 7th (D natural) keeps it from being fully major. The bridge shifts to minor but returns unresolved. Lennon was experimenting with Indian music's modal nature; the sitar reinforces the modal, non-functional harmony.

A foundational 12-bar blues in E. Every chord is a dominant 7th — even the I chord (E7), which creates constant tension that the form itself resolves through repetition rather than traditional harmonic resolution. The turnaround (last two bars) is a V-IV-I-V cycle.

SRV's guitar lines are pure minor pentatonic with blue notes (the b5) peppered in for that cry. The verse melody is a call-and-response between voice and guitar — the guitar answers every vocal phrase. Classic Texas blues shuffle with a driving, triplet-feel rhythm.

Minor blues rather than major — the changes are Bm7-Em7-F#m7 rather than the standard I7-IV7-V7. B.B. King's famous 'box' position in the minor pentatonic is all over this. Notice how he bends up to notes rather than playing them straight — that's the emotional core of blues guitar technique.

Built on a stop-time riff that gives the vocalist space to deliver lines. The harmonic motion is basic (E7) but the rhythmic structure is the teaching point — stop time means the band lays out while the soloist plays, then comes crashing back. Also a great example of the 'drone' approach to blues harmony.

The famous riff uses parallel perfect 4ths moving through the G minor pentatonic scale. Not traditional power chords (perfect 5ths) but perfect 4ths — which gives it a slightly more angular, modern sound. The simplicity is the lesson: three notes, parallel motion, unforgettable.

Page's riff is a pure E minor pentatonic statement. The one-note breakdown in the middle of the song (just E and a G) strips harmony to its bones, making the return of the full riff feel massive. A masterclass in tension and release through rhythmic and harmonic space.

McCartney's left hand creates a chromatic walking bass line while his melody sits above it — two voices in counterpoint on one acoustic guitar. The chords aren't simple open shapes; they're voice-led by the bass movement. A beautiful example of how chromatic bass lines create forward motion.

The progression G-B-C-Cm uses a major III chord (B major) borrowed from the parallel Mixolydian, then drops to a minor iv (Cm) — also borrowed, this time from the parallel minor. That Cm chord is the emotional gut-punch of the song. Modal interchange in its most visceral, accessible form.

The progression moves from F major through a Bb minor/F (minor iv chord borrowed from parallel minor) to create a bittersweet quality. The bass descends chromatically under static harmony in the bridge. McCartney originally called it 'Scrambled Eggs' — the harmonic sophistication snuck in naturally.

The I-V-vi-IV progression (C-G-Am-F) used as honestly and beautifully as it gets. The left hand's inversions — particularly the G/B — create a gentle stepwise bass line under the static right hand. Once you hear I-V-vi-IV, you hear it everywhere.

The intro drum pattern is in a 12/8 feel against a 4/4 chord sequence — a gentle polyrhythm. The chord changes use extensions (major 9ths, suspended chords) rather than plain triads, giving it a lush, orchestral quality even in a pop context.

The I-V-vi-IV loop (D-A-Bm-G) never resolves — it cycles indefinitely, which is exactly the emotional message. The bass pedals on D almost throughout while the chords shift above. The Edge's guitar adds sus4 notes that resolve to 3rds and back — constant, unresolved suspension.

The piano part is broken chords — not full chords, but individual notes in sequence (like an Alberti bass). The progression vi-IV-I-V (F#m-D-A-E) gives it a bittersweet major key with a minor starting point. The 'surprise' in this progression is that it starts on the vi and circles back to I — always returning, never fully arriving.

I-V-vi-IV (E-B-C#m-A) with an ascending stepwise piano intro that spells out the tonic chord. The bass line in the verse rises while the chords descend — contrary motion that creates momentum. A great example of how rhythmic and melodic treatment transform a simple progression.

The opening riff is built on the tritone interval (E to Bb) — the most dissonant interval in Western music. The famous E7#9 'Hendrix chord' contains both a major 3rd (G#) and a minor 3rd (G) simultaneously, creating deliberate tonal ambiguity. It's a dominant 7th chord with an augmented 9th — blues and major colliding.

Bm to F#7 is the key relationship — a minor i to a major V7 (from harmonic minor). The guitar solo is a two-part harmony between Felder and Walsh, structured almost classically. The chord sequence in the verse descends stepwise through natural minor with a borrowed V7 from harmonic minor.

The intro's chromatic descending bass line (A-G#-G-F#) under shifting chord colors is the harmonic engine. The piece builds from solo fingerpicked guitar to full band — a dynamic arc that's also a theory lesson in how texture and density create perceived harmonic weight.

The opening synth is an arpeggio in F Mixolydian — the flat 7th (Eb) is prominent. The verse and chorus stay in this mode, giving it a vast, open-sky quality. Pete Townshend's minimalist synth pattern over Keith Moon's explosive drums is a dynamic masterclass.

Slash's intro is a skip-string arpeggio pattern — moving between non-adjacent strings — that outlines a D major chord. The 'mistake' in the pattern (the C# that makes it a major 7th) is intentional. The verse guitar shifts to a rhythmic D5 pattern while the solo is pure D minor pentatonic despite being in D major — a blues tension.

concepts: E major to F major shift
context: Starts in E major with a clean fingerpicked arpeggio, then the chorus shifts to F# minor. The bridge drops to A minor — a sudden modal shift that feels like falling. Frusciante's chord voicings are always open and ringing, letting notes blur together. The outro solo is E minor pentatonic over shifting harmony.

The clavinet riff is an Eb minor pentatonic statement — one of the most recognizable hooks in popular music. The harmonic movement is minimal (mostly tonic and flat VII), keeping the groove in focus. The syncopated rhythmic placement of the riff is as important as the notes themselves.

album: Songs in the Key of Life
context: Pure major key joy. The harmonica melody is major pentatonic — the 4th and 7th are absent, keeping it bright and simple. Stevie's harmonica is in A but the song is in E, played with a 'cross harp' technique. A beautiful study in how major pentatonic melody creates an unambiguously happy emotional quality.

The bass line is the harmonic foundation — it moves chromatically in spots and defines the F# minor tonality more than the chords do. The chord progression is simple (F#m-B-E-A) but the bass line's rhythmic and melodic choices make it feel complex. The kick drum and bass are locked in perfect rhythmic union.

A jazz blues in F, but Hancock's Head Hunters version transformed it with a funk groove and beer bottle intro that defines the Mixolydian/blues hybrid. The main riff is pure F blues scale. Great example of how the same harmonic structure sounds completely different at different tempos and feels.

Opens on an Emaj9 chord — a major triad with a major 7th and a 9th stacked on top. This lush, open sound defined the album's sophisticated harmonic palette. The polyrhythmic percussion and multi-tracked vocal harmonies create density without complexity. Chord extensions (9ths, 11ths) are what separate soul from simple pop.

The most famous odd-meter jazz piece. The bass vamp (Ebm to Bbm7) cycles in 5/4 while Paul Desmond's alto saxophone melody sits comfortably on top. The key lesson: odd meters don't have to feel uncomfortable — Brubeck's vamp makes 5 feel as natural as 4.

album: The Dark Side of the Moon
context: The famous cash register/coins intro establishes a 7/4 rhythm before the bass riff enters — also in 7/4. The guitar solo switches to 4/4, creating a cross-rhythm effect. Pink Floyd's use of 7/4 feels natural because the bass riff is so strong and memorable.

The orchestral crescendo is a tone cluster — 40 musicians instructed to start on the lowest note of their instrument and go to the highest over 24 bars, as slowly as possible. The result is controlled noise. The final E major chord lasts 40+ seconds. Aleatory (chance-based) composition in a pop context.

Modulates through multiple key centers — Bb major in the ballad section, Eb major in the opera section, A major in the hard rock section. The Neapolitan chord (bII — a major chord built on the flattened second degree) appears in 'Galileo, Galileo, magnifico.' One of the most harmonically adventurous rock recordings ever made.

Stays in E natural minor (Aeolian) throughout. Manzarek's electric piano left hand establishes the harmony while his right hand plays the melody — one musician filling the bass, harmony, and melody roles simultaneously. The rain and thunder sound design reinforces the modal, moody quality.

Built on a bolero rhythm and the Phrygian mode — the flat 2nd gives it a Spanish/Moorish quality (influenced directly by Miles Davis's 'Sketches of Spain'). The song climaxes by resolving from the flat 2nd (G) up a half step to the tonic (F#). Phrygian's characteristic sound is that half-step resolution.

Monk's harmonic language at its most personal — diminished chords used as passing chords, chromatic approach notes, unexpected resolutions. The A section descends through a cycle of diminished and dominant chords. Miles Davis's 1955 recording with the original quintet is the definitive version harmonically.

The A section vamp sits on a Phrygian dominant scale (a Phrygian scale with a raised 3rd — the harmonic minor scale starting on its 5th degree). This gives it an 'exotic' Middle Eastern/Spanish quality. The B section is a conventional swing tune, making the contrast more dramatic.

Coltrane took a waltz from a musical and transformed it into a modal vehicle. The A section is E Dorian, the B section shifts to E major. His improvisation stays on one tonal center for minutes at a time — exploring the mode rather than navigating changes. This album popularized the soprano saxophone in jazz.

Opens with a free rubato intro borrowed from Rodrigo's Concierto de Aranjuez — pure Phrygian. Then launches into a driving A major latin jazz piece. The contrast between the dark Phrygian intro and the bright A major main theme is one of the most effective key-color contrasts in jazz.

A simple three-chord modal piece: Fm7 - Db7 - D7 - Fm7. The D7 is a tritone substitution for Ab7. Us3 famously sampled it for 'Cantaloop' in 1993. Harmonically simple but groove-forward — a reminder that rhythm and feel can carry a piece where harmony is minimal.

The ground bass (D-A-Bm-F#m-G-D-G-A) is the harmonic DNA of an enormous amount of Western pop music. Pachelbel's bass line repeats throughout while three violins play a canon above it. The I-V-vi-iii-IV-I-IV-V sequence moves through the key in a satisfying arc that always returns home.

Debussy's impressionist approach treats chords as colors rather than functions. The whole tone scale appears in the middle section — a symmetrical scale with no leading tone, creating a floating, unresolved quality. Every chord voicing is chosen for its sonic texture, not its functional relationship to neighboring chords.

Satie moves between Gmaj7 and Dmaj7 in the verse — parallel major 7th chords with no functional relationship. This non-functional, parallel harmonic movement was radical in 1888 and directly influenced impressionism, jazz, and ambient music. Brian Eno cited Satie as a key influence.
